topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp autumn air felt like a physical weight pressing down on Leo’s shoulders. He stood by the locker room door, the polished metal cold beneath his fingertips, staring at the team roster taped to the bulletin board. His name wasn't there. Not even a ghost of it. He replayed the final practice session in his head—the perfect sprint, the flawless execution—and suddenly, all the hard work felt like a cruel joke. A low, involuntary sound escaped his throat, a harsh, dry little cough that he quickly swallowed down. He turned away, the sound of muffled cheers from the other athletes grating on his nerves. He walked toward the exit, his jaw tight, the muscles in his neck corded with a simmering resentment that he refused to let anyone see.

[story 2]

"Guess I'll just go home then," Marcus muttered, the words barely audible over the general hubbub of the team gathering for a post-practice debrief. He shoved his hands deep into his pockets, the denim rough against his palms. Every glance from the coaches felt like a pointed accusation. He watched the designated starters high-five, their easy camaraderie a stark contrast to the hollow ache settling in his chest. He didn't look at them; instead, he focused intently on a scuff mark on the concrete floor, tracing its uneven edge with his eyes. A tightness gripped his chest, making each breath shallow and ragged. When the coach finally called his name, the sound didn't bring relief; it brought a sharp, stinging realization of where he truly stood.

[story 3]

"It's fine," Sarah said, forcing a smile that felt brittle around the edges. She adjusted her backpack strap, the movement stiff. Inside, her hands were clenched so tightly her knuckles were white. She looked at the team huddled together, their shared excitement radiating outward like heat. She felt a strange numbness spreading through her limbs, a heavy cloak of disappointment settling over her. When the coach announced the final lineup, Sarah simply nodded, a slow, deliberate movement. Her gaze swept over the assembled players, lingering just long enough to register the exclusion, before she turned and walked toward the exit, her steps measured and unnervingly steady.
